texts.label: text
texts.orig: In a landmark agreement in Paris in 2015, a global response to climate change was adopted in pursuing efforts to  limit  global  temperature  rise  to  1.5°C  above  pre-industrial  level.  Recently  at  the  climate  summit  COP27 1 , alarming trends in current CO2 emissions were presented and global temperature is predicted to rise over 1.5 °C already in 2030. The consequential "code red situation for humanity" is declared by Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) in 2022 calling for the most drastic cuts in CO2 emissions to avoid dangerous anthropogenic interference with the climate system. These high levels of ambitions require an acceleration in effort investment in emission reduction technologies of all types across all sectors . 2

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Carbon  Capture  Utilization  and  Storage  (CCUS) has  been  a  tried  and  tested  solution  for  conservative industries that rely on retrofits to existing infrastructure and meeting CO2 emission targets at the same time. The shipping industry, that is due to face stringent regulations, is no exception to favor CCUS solutions as they offer near-medium term solutions given their technological maturity . They are also expected to offer long term solutions to the recently inducted hydrocarbon fleet with decades of service left .  However, significant challenges  like  energy,  environmental  and  footprint  efficiency  of  CO2  capture  technologies,  need  alternate solutions and infrastructure for CO2 handling inhibits CCUS deployment in this hard-to-abate CO2 sector.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: AMbCS brings in 7 partners from 3 countries ranging from technology developers, engineering suppliers and end users in this project and builds a holistic solution favorable for rapid deployment of CCUS in Shipping Industry. The AMbCS project will demonstrate a novel membrane technology at TRL 6 which is beneficial for CO2 capture in ships due to its compact size (low footprint), absence of moving parts, modularity (easy startup/shut down and dynamic response to changes in flue gas conditions) and absence of emissions from harmful chemicals compared to solvent technology.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To curb the associated growth in greenhouse gas emissions (GHG) in the Shipping sector, the International Maritime Organization (IMO) adopted reduction in GHG emissions in shipping by 50% by 2050 and subsequently agreed to 40% reduction in its carbon intensity by 2030 compared to 2018 levels. However, IMO's data collection system only accounts for two-thirds of the international fleet and their strategy mainly relies on efficiency increase requirements  (by  changes  in  operation,  fuel  type)  through  Energy  Efficiency  Design  Index  monitoring  for regulatory compliance, starting January 2023. It is already predicted that about 75% of the global fleet of bulk carriers and tankers will not be able to comply with new technical metrics mandated by the IMO 4 . Additionally, their  decarbonization  strategy  of  transition  to  zero-carbon  fuel  is  rendered  challenging  in  the  near  term. Nevertheless, despite the rough unfavourable pathways suggested by IMO to meet their 40% carbon intensity reduction target in 2030, the emissions still don't align with the 1.5°C target (Figure 1). Drastic regulations need to be in place if the sector is to contribute to climate neutrality . 5

texts.orig: shipping industry is on the lookout for the most economic solutions for the near, medium and long-term emission reductions. Zero-emission fuel like H2 and ammonia technologies, although promising, are hindered by the low maturity  of  technology,  lack  of  supporting  infrastructure  and significant  renewal  of  vessel  fleet  which  has considerable lifetime left in service before 2030. On the other hand, for inland navigation, although many of the barges opt for alternative fuel technologies including electricity, battery and H2, the majority of the fleet are still expected to remain fossil fuel powered. This is mainly due to the average lifetime of the barge fleet being higher than sea-going vessels slowing new fleet induction, range-restrictions of battery-powered barges, low maturity of H2 and chemical-powered shipping technology and the associated safety and emission risks within EU waters.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Carbon  Capture  Utilization  and  Storage  (CCUS) has  been  a  tried  and  tested  solution  for  conservative industries. They  are  expected  to offer  long-term  solutions  to  recently  inducted  hydrocarbon  fleet  with decades of service left . Additionally,  with  growing  infrastructure  for  CO2  in  the  EU  captured  at  large-point sources through pipelines, ports via massive projects like Longship and Northern Lights (NO), Porthos (NL) and Antwerp@C, Kairos@C (BE), CCUS can be a viable option for the shipping industry to reduce its emissions. Recent studies have also proven the feasibility of CCUS (post-combustion) in shipping but mainly limited to solvent-based  and  adsorption-based  systems  (Value  maritime) 7 ,  with  many  projects  ongoing  like  EverLoNG (funded by ACT3), while membranes for CO2 capture on ships remain to be demonstrated.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Despite significant efforts, CCUS deployment is slower than required in the shipping industry. From the industrial viewpoint, the current bottlenecks in the deployment of CCUS solutions include- availability of space, excess electric energy for the retrofits decreasing voyage efficiency, emission restrictions associated with capture plants and challenges in offloading, transport, utilization and/or storage of captured CO2.

texts.orig: Recently, SINTEF Energy also conducted a feasibility study of carbon capture processes for marine applications as a part of their CCShip project to identify further challenges. texts.label: text
texts.orig: One main challenge for full scale deployment of CCUS in shipping is the lack of conducive pathways to Storage or Utilization after the capture brick. Depending on the use of CO2, the full chain downstream of capture process requires fine purification (to get to necessary CO2 specifications) and CO2 handling solutions which plays an interim role onboard before the CO2 is offloaded permanently. Given the complex inter-dependency of these processes  in  relevance  to  the  end-user  requirements  for  emission  reduction,  tailored  solutions  are  often necessary requiring rigorous assessments to result in favorable solutions.

The  overall methodology devised to meet the goals of the project spread across different WPs is outlined below.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Flue gas emissions on board ships are widely variable depending on the engine load, fuel type and the inlet air characteristics.  The  exhaust  gas  pressure  is  close  to  atmospheric  pressure  in  most  cases,  with  CO2 concentrations ranging from 3-10% Dry vol. Within the AMbCS project, two emission sources will be considered; Barge Helsinki, where the demonstration is taking place, operated by VIC and chartered by Shell, and Vessel PAAE280158 that will be used for feasibility study, owned by GOT. The flue gas conditions are detailed in Table 1.

texts.text: 1.4.3. Analysis of fine purification of CO2 and CO2 handling solutions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Exploring  fine  purification  methods  of  CO2  will  include  conceptual  feasibility  studies  and  TEA  of  secondary purification  stage  downstream  of  the  membrane  capture  unit.  Different  technologies  include  small  scale liquefaction  and  storage  under  different  pressures,  Joule-Thompson  assisted  liquefaction  and  storage,  an additional membrane stage coupled with adsorption-assisted high-pressure storage (highly porous MOF) 11 . In addition to the flue gas characteristics and CO2 capture requirements, additional constraints such as available space, waste heat, electric power, quality of CO2 for selected CO2 handling case, average voyage time and distance, geolocations etc . will be considered for assessment of these purification methods.
texts.text: Exploring  fine  purification  methods  of  CO2  will  include  conceptual  feasibility  studies  and  TEA  of  secondary purification  stage  downstream  of  the  membrane  capture  unit. In addition to the flue gas characteristics and CO2 capture requirements, additional constraints such as available space, waste heat, electric power, quality of CO2 for selected CO2 handling case, average voyage time and distance, geolocations etc . will be considered for assessment of these purification methods.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Different CO2 handling solutions are being considered depending on the use of CO2 12 . For instance, in the case of storage; offloading at port for pipeline transport, offloading at a temporary midway port followed by exclusive ship transport of CO2, offloading at an offshore, subsea injection point where CO2 is reinjected for EOR or for storage, offloading into temporary storage for onward transport to an injection site (CO2 collection hubs), moored storage at an injection site, or another port connected to an injection site. The project will also consider a much ambitious solution where offshore ship-to-ship transfer (de-bunkering of CO2) is made. Such a CO2 handling case, although challenging, is viable for cargo vessels and ships that have long voyages.

texts.orig: The AMbCS consortium acknowledges that STEM should focus on gender neutrality. Equality between women, non-binary and men in research and development is essential for optimum growth both in industry and research organizations. Gender equality must be promoted within our field of study and in others. This is believed to both strengthen efficiency and improve quality. It is known that participation of women and non-binary in industrial and academic environments related to the project is still low due to various reasons such as low numbers of women/non-binary students in engineering and science disciplines, difficulties in finding solutions for maintaining both  work  and  family,  and  the  lack  of  networks  to  support  women/non-binary  to  develop  their  careers. The technical objectives within the AMbCS project do not involve a gender aspect. Still, wherever possible, the project will  seek  to  promote  women's/non-binary  representation  in  science,  engineering,  and  technology.  Sex  and gender will be addressed as a variable during project execution for those tasks in which it may represent an influence regarding the pursued outcome. AMbCS will encourage female researchers, not only those that worked on this project but also generally speaking, to be present in other projects and it will support gender equity by offering  work  to  young  females  for  doctorate  and  academic  positions.  With  these  measures,  AMbCS  will contribute to eradicate gender-based disparities and promote gender equality in accordance with the EU Strategy for equality between women and men (COM (2010) 491 final).

The open science approach will ensure reproducibility, replicability, and interoperability of data. The FAIR principles will ensure that the results of the project will be reproducible - the research design and the methodologies will be specified, and any negative results or barriers and how to mitigate against those will be made available. texts.orig: The partners will follow the good practices of Data Management in Open Science, which will be ensured by the project lead Aqualung. The relevant partners will discuss and determine whether any confidentiality constraints will be applied to certain data that will be made accessible to a wider audience including the public and end users, who are outside of the project. The data outputs made available will be documented and made accessible through the most suitable channels, to ensure data protection, discoverability, and correct interpretation to expert and non-expert users. The data,  results  and  publications  will  be  shared  via  open  access  repositories  (e.g., Zenodo, the INRAE, Dataverse, and Gitlab forge). The NTNU designated servers, which are called "AURORA" - Archive Upload Research Objects for Retrieval and Alteration, will be used so that the "lifetime" of the archived data set(s) can be custom defined.

The partners from each country provide complementary expertise to arrive at a successful full chain solution for accelerated deployment in ambitious time scale.

texts.orig: The EU, as the world's second-largest economy, is attempting to become the first climate neutral continent by 2050. 19  The scale up of an environmentally friendly and energy-efficient membrane-based full chain CCUS solution has the potential to support the decarbonization transition of the EU, diversify supply chains into new industries -- preserve jobs, stimulate economic growth, and thus contribute to the development of Europe as a global leader in the clean, competitive economy of the future.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The main objective of the AMbCS project is to develop an environmentally friendly and energy efficient CCUS solution for the shipping industry. Using membrane technology, CO2 can be filtered from flue gases of inland and marine vessels. The project will investigate the potential of such solution and demonstrate it within the value chain, from the safe capture of CO2 in an industrial ship environment, interim storage of the captured CO2 on board, to safe unloading of CO2 from seagoing vessels and e.g., by using inland tankers developed for that purpose and transport of CO2. Thus, the project can contribute to the development of a  CCUS value chain, allowing both industry and maritime and inland navigation to efficiently reduce CO2 emissions.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In 2018, The EU shipping industry supported €54 billion contribution to GDP. The industry employed 685,000 highly skilled people and through supply chain and worker spending multiplier impact, its support of jobs rises to over 2.0 million. 20  Yet, shipping is also a significant source of greenhouse gas emissions (GHG), accounting for around 3% of global GHG emissions annually. At the EU level it is a substantial source of CO2, representing 3 to 4% of the EU's total CO2 emissions, or over 144 million tons of CO2 in 2019 and expected to increase. 21 There are about 6,000 inland barges in Europe that will need alternative ways to decarbonize over the coming decade. In addition, the shipping industry will be subject to the EU ETS System starting 2024, which works on the cap-and-trade principle and sets a total amount of certain greenhouse gases that can be emitted by the operators  included  in  the  system. This  will  be  an  extra  incentive  for  shipping  to  integrate  carbon  emissionreducing techniques on board.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The shipping sector is globally considered "hard to abate", it is one of the sectors that is difficult to decarbonise. The full value chain for alternative fuels such as Hydrogen, Green Methanol or Ammonia is not expected to be mature in the next 10 years. Furthermore, it has taken decades for major energy sources - fossil fuels, to provide significant share of global supply. For instance, natural gas took 50 years to provide 20% of global supply of energy and modern renewables still supplies 5% of global demand. 22   Combining CO2 capture with alternative renewable fuels (biomass, e-fuels) can also create a negative CO2 power source while requiring the least amount of infrastructure / equipment capital costs associated with new fuel sources.

texts.orig: This reality is diametrically opposed to the climate goals of Fit For 55 and the European Green Deal, so additional and rapid efforts and investments are needed to be able to drastically reduce CO2 and other GHG emissions. Consequently, CCUS is considered as a promising solution for both land-based industry and shipping for the hard-to-abate sectors. CCUS is considered to play a crucial role in meeting global climate targets. In order to limit global temperature, rise to 1.5°C, leading organizations including the International Energy Agency (IEA), International Renewable Energy Agency (IRENA) and Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) have all produced long-term energy outlooks that rely on a rapid expansion of CCUS. 23

texts.orig: The global gas separation membranes market size24 is projected to reach $1.2 billion by 2026 from $0.9 billion in 2021, at a CAGR of 6.7% during the forecast period. The European membrane market size was valued at around 420 million Euros in 2020 and the market revenue is expected to grow at 6.83% through 2021 to 2027. Various  regulations  regarding  carbon  capture  and  storage  are  driving  the  growth  of  the  gas  separation membranes market. The development of mixed matrix membranes is an opportunity for the gas  separation membranes market growth. Commercialization of new and advanced membrane systems are also projected to boost market growth of gas separation membranes. CO2 removal is the fastest growing segment in this market which is being targeted in this project. Membrane technology in CCS has a significant carbon mitigation potential, which makes it attractive option for the shipping industry.

texts.orig: The membrane technology has great competitive advantages and scaling up potential. The technology has potentially no emissions from harmful chemicals and the mass transfer is gas-solid, therefore no issues with sea swell resonances in the capture process. Moreover, the membrane CCUS technology is very well suited for installations on vessels, both maritime vessels and barges due to absence of moving parts and modularity. First, the system can be retrofitted on existing vessels, not only installed on newly built ones. Second, it is a compact technology fit for a crowded vessel, which makes it safe to operate in a limited space such as an inland barge engine room. Third, the technology is also easy to operate, unexpensive and quicker to install. The modular design means, it can be scaled up easily from a cost and footprint perspective. texts.orig: As the infrastructure on handling CO2 in ports develop, there is an exceptionally large market potential by 2030 due to the ease of offloading CO2 as well as the lack of retrofit required by shipowners. This infrastructure is expected to develop first in Europe including around the Northern Light facility in Norway and the port of Antwerp. By combining a feasible carbon capture system on vessels and the logistics for handling the CO2, will provide a significant market potential given the size of the shipping market in Norway, Sweden, and Belgium, which are all significant shipping nations domestically and internationally.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The project will ensure the reproducibility and reproducibility through open science approach (see Section 1.6  for  more  information) while  building  trust  within  the  shipping  industry  on  the  techniques/innovations developed in the project through sharing of knowledge and increasing market-uptake. A carbon capture system must address limited space and weight restrictions. Installation and maintenance must be done while in port and the vessel is offshore. The safety of operation is also stricter than in most industrial locations. Thus, this project will enable the market to see how the technology is installed and operates on a vessel. It will enable the customer to evaluate CAPEX, OPEX, efficiency, durability, and performance. As a demo project, it will enable us to address any problems that occur during the implementation. The proposed solutions enable involvement of research units,  technology  provider,  engineering  supplier  and  end  users  to  innovative  membrane  development.  The demonstrator approach will enable to establish the documentation of the needed KPIs for the deployment. The demos and studies will serve as references for future installation, saving time, resources, and costs in developing the technology.

It will enable the customer to evaluate CAPEX, OPEX, efficiency, durability, and performance. As a demo project, it will enable us to address any problems that occur during the implementation. The proposed solutions enable involvement of research units,  technology  provider,  engineering  supplier  and  end  users  to  innovative  membrane  development.  The demonstrator approach will enable to establish the documentation of the needed KPIs for the deployment. The demos and studies will serve as references for future installation, saving time, resources, and costs in developing the technology.

texts.orig: The project partners also aim to explore, beyond the timeline of the project, new markets such as maritime CO2 transport. Companies that emit a lot of CO2, such as the cement or petrochemical industries, will be able to capture and store their CO2 in the near future. The project partners such as Victrol, aims to use a fleet of "zeroemission vessels" to transport this CO2 to the Western European seaports, where the product will be temporarily stored for further transport by ocean-going vessel or pipeline to final underground storage sites or for e-fuel utilization.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Maritime vessels consume 20-100 tons of gasoil per day according to type, size, and market activity. This implies that  onboard  CO2  storage  becomes  quite  a  challenge  (fuel  consumption  multiplied  by  about  factor  2.65) especially as voyage distances increase. Therefore, CO2 collection can be considered as a new potential market. For instance, Victrol's CO2 tankers can recharge stored CO2 when maritime vessels call at a Benelux port to transport it to temporary storage sites. The same CO2tankers can also be used to connect the major industrial hubs inland to those same interim storage sites in the seaports. Thanks to this project, the partners could tap into an entire new market that has the potential to offer shipping a climate-friendly fuel alternative for the next 20 years.

texts.orig: Task 1-1: Upgrading membrane modules for steam operation (Task leader: NTNU; Participants: ACC, SINTEF)
texts.text: Task 1-1: Upgrading membrane modules for steam operation (Task leader: NTNU; Participants: ACC, SINTEF)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The existing module design from ACC will be tuned to facilitate the use of steam as sweep gas. Current modules with an area of 5 m 2  will be updated with high temperature (upto 110 ° C) sealing to avoid volume expansion and tested for mechanical and performance stability with steam exposure. Coating chemistry (changes in protective layer design) will be made if needed. NTNU will use their advanced coating and module fabrication facility to upgrade the modules with the help of ACC and SINTEF. The modules will be individually validated for performances using steam both as sweep and as sweep-vacuum combination.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: End  users  will  prepare  by  gathering  site  plans,  zone  maps,  utility  services,  tie  in  points,  standards  and operational constraints to identify infrastructural requirements and ensure uninterrupted safe demonstration activities. Infrastructure preparation like flue gas slip stream, installation of the utilities such as cooling water, steam, instrument air, heat tracings, drainage, electricity lines, vent lines, constructional services, implementation of the gas and fire detection systems and communication of the control system if needed will be prepared in close contact with AND and ACC. The end users will determine dedicated technical personnel on each site for project follow-up, Hazard and Operability Study (HAZOP) meetings, technical visits, Factory Acceptance Test (FAT), commissioning including Site Acceptance Test (SAT) and training  of  the  site  operators.  The  site  owners  collaborating  with  technology  developers  will  follow  all necessary paperworks in export/import of pilot units including insurances and fit-onboard certifications. SINTEF, ACC, AND and all the end-users will establish together a detailed test matrix (D2-1) to define the list of tests with their characteristics, objectives, estimated duration, required resources, associated risks and their mitigation measures. Based on this procedure, ACC will establish a list of specific instrumentation to efficiently monitor the tests' performances.

texts.orig: Task 2-3: Pilot demonstration (Task leader: VIC, ACC, AND, SINTEF and GOT)
texts.text: Task 2-3: Pilot demonstration (Task leader: VIC, ACC, AND, SINTEF and GOT)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The pilot demonstration is planned onboard freshwater barges powered by diesel (VIC) in two phases with an optional interval of 1-3 weeks in between for shipping and repair/replacements of parts/membranes. The first phase of campaign at VIC is expected to last for 2 weeks starting with parameter testing with personnel from ACC onboard (2 weeks). With the gained familiarity of operation, the second phase of campaign will follow comprising of unattended long-term operation (2 weeks to 3 months) and shutdown (1 week). The second test campaign is mainly to demonstrate the system in rather simulated restricted environment in a sea-going passenger/cargo vessel (mimicking GOT case with respect to restrictions in long voyages) with no quick access to ports and hence planned to last only 1-2 weeks. The campaigns target successful demonstration of membrane-based CO2 up-concentration system at steady state for safety, reliability, flexibility and efficiency. A cumulative 2400 hours of total operation is planned accounting to more than 5 tCO2 removed from both phase of test campaigns.

texts.text: Task 3-1: Full-scale process design and optimization for three user cases (Task leader: AND, NTNU, VIC, ACC, SINTEF)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The processes designed in Task 1.4 will be updated with validated performance data from pilot test campaigns in WP2 and optimized for final process designs (D1.4) for three case studies - Inland EU freshwater cargo (VIC), short range ferries and shipping in Nordic waters (represented by GoT), long haul container cargo vessels (represented by GoT). VIC will also study the business case of debunkering temporarily stored CO2 in  large  vessels  close  to  sea  using  their  CO2  transport  barges  to  aid  offshore  CO2  collection  and transportation  to  storage  ports  (contribution  to  Longship,  pipelines  collection  nodes  etc).  Optimized solutions for each end user for different locations will be sought. AND will also perform a water footprint analysis to assess benefits and risks of using steam as sweep.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: With optimized process for each user case in Task 3-1, a full mass and energy balance of the AMbCS process will be performed, the main equipment are defined and sized, and a layout of the resulting installation is proposed.  Then,  the  investment  and  operation  costs  (NOAK)  are  estimated.  Finally,  KPIs  such  as  the overall energy consumption per tonne of CO2 captured and the CO2 Avoidance Cost (CAC) are computed. Potential technical and cost-based hotspots/and potential ways of improvement are identified. The work will focus first on the TEA of the plant. Afterwards, the whole supply chain will also be considered by means of  CO2 transport  and  storage (CO2  handling) Identified  optimal for  different  cases.  TEA studies will  be carried out for three scenarios at three geographical locations.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Task 3-3: Roadmap for scaleup and extension possibilities (Task leader: ACC, VIC, GOT, AND, SINTEF) The roadmap will identify the bottlenecks and subsequent steps to bring the AMbCS solutions to market wide deployment.  Infrastructure  requirements  such  as  CO2  collection  nodes  at  vicinity,  access  to  pipelines, regulatory challenges will be identified, and gap analysis will be performed to establish a strong business case for AMbCS solutions. Replication possibilities to other users in international waters will be analysed.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A  large  multidisciplinary  project  like AMbCS  requires  an  efficient  and  well-defined  management  structure  to ensure execution within the resources and time frame available. A general coordination and organisation plan is proposed together with specific management plans concerning quality assurance, risks mitigation, intellectual property management and data management, dissemination and exploitation. Given the variety of players in the consortium, a structure is proposed with a project coordinator which will be assisted by the Project Management Team  (PMT),  including  WP  Leaders  and  a  Quality Assurance  manager,  Quality Assurer  and  administrative personnel, responsible for administrative, legal and financial aspects of the Grant Agreement (Figure 12) The End-user Advisory Board (EAB) will be the complementary steering body for the execution of the project, while the General Assembly (GA) will be the highest decision-making body. To facilitate communication between the project contributors, Progress Meetings will be organized on a regular basis. In addition, an Innovation manager and Exploitation manager are assigned in the project, while disseminations are managed from WP4. Disputes and conflicts are resolved at the next higher or progressively higher levels in the AMbCS organization,

texts.label: text
texts.orig: AMbCS brings together a technology provider (ACC), three established research organisations (SINTEF, SER and NTNU), an engineering company (AND) and three end-users (VIC, GoT) onboard to develop a holistic CCUS solution for shipping industry.
texts.text: AMbCS brings together a technology provider (ACC), three established research organisations (SINTEF, SER and NTNU), an engineering company (AND) and three end-users (VIC, GoT) onboard to develop a holistic CCUS solution for shipping industry.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: ACC  is  rapidly  evolving  to  accelerate  Carbon  Capture,  Utilisation  and  Sequestration  (CCUS)  projects  by providing cost effective solutions for a wide range of industrial emitters and fuel sources across the spectrum, from natural gas to cement production. Aqualung seeks to create value and mitigate capital risk for our clients through a tolling model.  ACC will deliver pilot units to both Standard lithium in the US and Nordkalk in SE before Q1 2023.

Aqualung seeks to create value and mitigate capital risk for our clients through a tolling model.  ACC will deliver pilot units to both Standard lithium in the US and Nordkalk in SE before Q1 2023.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Mr. Henrik Utvik is the CTO of the company with 8+ years in O&G industry and will be the coordinator of the project. Dr. Saravanan Janakiram , R&D and product manager will be the Quality Assurer of the project. He is one of the inventors of the patented Aqualung technology and has 6+ years of experience in CO2 separation membranes and is the recipient of Patrick Meares best PhD in Membrane science by European Membrane Society in 2022.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: SINTEF is a broadly based, multidisciplinary research concern that possesses international top-level expertise in technology, medicine and the social sciences. SINTEF is an independent, non-commercial organization. has a  broad  experience  of  different  CO2  capture  technologies  utilizing  absorption  solvent,  membranes  and  solid sorbents through a high number of both national and international projects, such as the CCS national research centres BIGCCS and NCCS, H2020 GENESIS, FP7 HIPERCAP, FP7 CACHET II. Dr. Thijs Peters (WP5 lead) has over 15 years' experience in development and characterization of membrane for gas separation, Dr. Luca Ansaloni has over 10 years' experience in development of membrane and membrane materials for CO2 capture applications.

Dr. Thijs Peters (WP5 lead) has over 15 years' experience in development and characterization of membrane for gas separation, Dr. Luca Ansaloni has over 10 years' experience in development of membrane and membrane materials for CO2 capture applications.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: SINTEF ER has a leading national position as an RTD provider, delivering innovative and sustainable energy solutions through, for example, CCS, energy efficiency in industry, and hydrogen. In particular, SINTEF ER has deep and broad experience in the field of CCS from 2001 including CO2 capture processes and their integration, CO2 transport and CCS value chain analysis. Based on the wide expertise in CCS, SINTEF ER has actively led many Norwegian  and  international  CCS  project  such  as  the  FME  centres  BIGCCS  and  NCCS,  as  well  as European projects such as H2020 CEMCAP, H2020 CHEERS, FP7 IMPACTS, ACT Elegancy, etc. Dr Rahul Anantharaman has over 15 years of experience in working with CO2 capture process design and integration. Mr. Simon Roussanaly has over 10 years of experience in CO2 value chain analysis and techno-economic analysis of CO2 value chains.

He is currently working together with ACC in the fine tuning of their membrane up-scaling endeavour. Prof. Liyuan Deng is  the  leader  of  the  membrane  research  laboratory  at  NTNU. texts.orig: VIC is a Belgian shipping operator, mainly active in river transports of heavy cargoes, bulk oil and gas (LNG) and is providing solutions for inland transportation of CO2 (CCUS infrastructure) and climate neutral energy. VIC has been very active in CCUS and has recently completed full design of CO2 barge for Co. In this project, WP2 will be led by Mr. Fred Selhorst , who is currently Business development manager and experienced in CCUS for the barge industry.

table_cells.text: Build an innovation ecosystem that fosters capacity building at all governance and actor levels, faster market diffusion, upscaling and replication…

AMbCS project brings in the right expertise onboard with a technology provider at high TRL, three research institutes, one engineering company with significant client network and two end-users to upgrade, upscale, demonstrate, assess, and realise the technology immediately after the project.
Accelerate clean energy technology development and transition to the widely decarbonized energy systems through demonstration, innovation in technology development, and integration and system change

AMbCS project provides short to medium and long term solutions for meeting the decarbonisation goals of the shipping industry. Working with high TRL demonstrations as a part of the full chain is a leverage for AMbCS to expedite integration of CCUS in a rather conservative end-user segment that has stringent emission targets in 2030

.. projects that have a significant bearing on accelerating the technologies and provide results showing significant CO 2 reduction by 2030 and demonstrate a contribution to the climate and clean transition.

Challenges in emission reduction technologies onboard ships significantly outweigh point sources due to space, cost, energy and environmental constraints. AMbCS offers membrane-based CCUS solutions which are optimal for small-medium scale installations and plans to overcome key hurdles in the full value chain that prevent deployment. The results are expected to expedite clean transport and contribute significant reductions by 2030.

table_cells.text: The Emissions Trading System (ETS) Directive revised to include the maritime sector 15

Maritime transport in the EU is proposed to be subject to the ETS Directive as from 1st of January 2023, and ships currently reporting emissions under the EU's MRV (Monitoring, Reporting and Verification) regulation would have to purchase CO 2 emission credits, affecting voyages into (or out of) the EU, including stays at EU ports. Allocation of ETS allowances would account of 100% of emissions from intra-EU voyages and berth activities in EU ports and of 50% of emissions for voyages entering or leaving an EU port. Responsibility and payment of the allowances would fall on the ship's commercial operator. There would also be a gradual phase-in period beginning with the requirement to surrender 20% of allowance for verified emissions in 2023 and rising gradually to 100% by 2025. Shipping companies failing to comply will be fined and may eventually be banned from EU waters.
AMbCS targets accelrated CCUS deployment in Shipping industry to realize emission targets as early as 2030 with a technolgoy fully developed in the EU.

On 23 March, the co-legislators agreed a new EU regulation ensuring that the greenhouse gas intensity of fuels used by the shipping sector will gradually decrease over time, by2% in 2025 to as much as 80% by 2050.
CO 2 emissions are expected to remain a significant share of GHG emissions in shipping industry calling for CCUS

This proposed regulation repeals an existing Directive and would require that EU member states adopt national policy frameworks to expand their refueling infrastructures, so that availability of liquefied natural gas (LNG) and hydrogen is guaranteed in all EUports by 2025, and of shore-side electricity supply by 2030.
Although AMbCS doesn't target H 2 , medium to long haul vessels are still expected to use LNG which is clean burning and high energy density neccessitating CCUS

An update to the existing EU Framework on taxation of energy products is under negotiation and provides that bunker fuel sold in the EU for intra-EU waterborne navigation will be taxed. The bunker fuel for extra-EU waterborne navigation could remain tax-free according to the discretion of the member state. Sustainable and alternative fuels will be exempt from tax for a period of ten years.
In addition to the ETS, CCUS implementation is expected

table_cells.text: Key exploitable outcomes within and beyond the project timeline
Dissemination and communication activities and target groups

Aqualung is aiming to accelerate the commercialization of advanced membrane based CCUS solution for Shipping industry projects. The technology has already been tested at industrial sites onshore. This project will enable the market to see how it is installed and operates on a vessel. It will enable the customer to evaluate CAPEX, OPEX, efficiency, durability, and performance.

Dissemination activities of Aqualung will be aimed at creating awareness about the opportunities of this technology, as well as communicating the successes and milestones achieved in the project. Besides the usual activity on social media and the project website, this will be done for example by talks at conferences on AMbCS solutions as well as by publishing press releases and actively reaching out to attract interest of potential scale up and off-take partners. The project also generates new IP which can add to existing patent family of Aqualung. Target Audience: Potential partners (national, (inter)national; commercial, non-commercial), technology adopters (inter)national; policy makers (national, EU); media; non-governmental organisations or groups and wider public.

table_cells.text: If the revised budget estimated at the end of the basic design exceeds the initial budget, two mitigation measures can be applied in the following order:
1) Industry can take up to 10% of the initial budget.
2) reduce pilot-scale to 26 NM3/h or simplify the design.
To address these risks, the following measures will be taken: 1) Implement several control gates in the project to monitor delays early and take rapid actions.
2) Parallelize connection work to cement plant simultaneously as packages design & manufacturing.
3) Long-term tests will be reconsidered for the duration if necessary.
Pilot plant not able to run for 2400 hrs
1) Keep running the plant after the project. 2) As a minimum, the pilot should run 1200 hrs during the project, task for

The effect of the social activities in the project will be seen in long term. No significant improvements expected during the project. Outreach activities aimed at young generation which will make decision in 10-20 years.
Pressure fluctuations from emission source leads to spikes in system pressure/flow causing system shutdown
PID control loops will be tuned during SAT. If large fluctuations are expected this should be identified in the project basis of design
Water cycle/footprint not in favour of using steam
The steam cycle inside the capture unit is expected to be closed loop with very minimal water make up. Additional measures like waste water treatment might be considered if needed
System electrical load exceeds available capacity of site
System will be designed to utilise steam as sweep gas to reduce power requirements of rotating equipment
Elevated temperatures required for steam operation has negative
The membranes will be operated at high T in lab conditions and with expsoure of impurities to check for any potential degradations.

table_cells.text: Particulate buildup/fouling leading to reduced performance or blocking of the filters/membranes resulting in decreased performance through the process
Ensure periodic monitoring of the differential pressure across the filter and across the membrane stages
Insufficient water/CO 2 content leads to drying of the membranes and reduced performance
Humidification will be included in system design if requried by feed gas spec
HVAC designed for sufficient air changes if containerised, fixed site environmental gas monitoring to be provided, personal gas monitors to be worn by operators.
Acidic condensate is not able to be discharged to site drainage system
Dosing package can be included in scope
Loss of utilities leading to system shutdown
Delays or supply shortages for key equipment items leads to delays during construction
Long lead items are identified and procured early in design phase
Ensure all necessary customs clearance documentation is in order

CCS deployment depends almost entirely on policy and global agreements for CO 2 reductions. CCS projects have to be subsidised for the next many decades in order to be realised. That in no way detracts from the importance of the task. We will demonstrate what can be achieved if there is the desire

The partners in the consortium have complementary and overlapping competence. The tasks will be redistributed between the partners. Alternatively, a partner replacement will be found. Excellent networking by key partners allows to look for substitutes.
